Angular Momentum and Term Symbols

What you will learn

  • Total orbital angular momentum L^=∑i=1Nl^i\hat{L} = \sum_{i=1}^N \hat{l}_iL^=∑i=1N​l^i​ quantifies the combined angular momentum of all electrons, with quantized values of LLL ranging from ∣l1−l2∣|l_1 - l_2|∣l1​−l2​∣ to l1+l2l_1 + l_2l1​+l2​.

  • Total spin angular momentum S^=∑i=1Ns^i\hat{S} = \sum_{i=1}^N \hat{s}_iS^=∑i=1N​s^i​ follows a similar quantization rule, with possible SSS values for two electrons being 1 (triplet) or 0 (singlet).

  • The total angular momentum J^=L^+S^\hat{J} = \hat{L} + \hat{S}J^=L^+S^ combines orbital and spin contributions, with JJJ taking values from ∣L−S∣|L - S|∣L−S∣ to L+SL + SL+S.

  • Term symbols are written as 2S+1LJ^{2S+1}L_J2S+1LJ​, where 2S+12S+12S+1 is the spin multiplicity, LLL is the total orbital momentum (S, P, D, F, ...), and JJJ is the total angular momentum.

  • Hund’s rules state that (1) the highest spin multiplicity lies lowest in energy, (2) for the same SSS, the highest LLL is lowest, and (3) for the same SSS and LLL, the smallest JJJ is lowest for a less-than-half-filled subshell, while the largest JJJ is lowest for a more-than-half-filled subshell.

  • Spin-orbit interaction couples L⃗\vec{L}L and S⃗\vec{S}S via H^SO=A L^⃗⋅S^⃗\hat{H}_{SO} = A\,\vec{\hat{L}} \cdot \vec{\hat{S}}H^SO​=AL^⋅S^, causing fine-structure splitting, especially in heavier atoms.

  • Selection rules for allowed transitions are Δl=±1\Delta l = \pm 1Δl=±1, ΔL=0,±1\Delta L = 0, \pm 1ΔL=0,±1 (except L=0→L=0L = 0 \to L = 0L=0→L=0), ΔJ=0,±1\Delta J = 0, \pm 1ΔJ=0,±1 (except J=0→J=0J = 0 \to J = 0J=0→J=0), and ΔS=0\Delta S = 0ΔS=0.

  • In light atoms, Russell-Saunders (LSLSLS) coupling applies, so LLL, SSS, and JJJ are all good quantum numbers; in heavier atoms spin-orbit coupling dominates and JJJ becomes the only good quantum number.

  • The ground-state term symbol of an atom is determined using Hund’s rules; for example He (1s21s^21s2) has term symbol 1^11S0_00​, while C (1s22s22p21s^2 2s^2 2p^21s22s22p2) has term symbol 3^33P0_00​.

The Vector Model for Adding Angular Momenta¶

In many-electron atoms, each electron has both orbital and spin angular momenta. First, for simplicity, consider only the total orbital angular momentum operator:

L^=∑i=1Nl^i{\hat{L} = \sum\limits_{i=1}^{N} \hat{l}_i}L^=i=1∑N​l^i​
(1)

where NNN is the number of electrons and l^i\hat{l}_il^i​ is the angular momentum operator for electron iii. The projection operator along the zzz-axis is then:

L^z=∑i=1Nl^z,i{\hat{L}_z = \sum\limits_{i=1}^{N} \hat{l}_{z,i}}L^z​=i=1∑N​l^z,i​
(2)
ML=∑i=1Nmi{M_L = \sum\limits_{i=1}^{N}m_i}ML​=i=1∑N​mi​
(3)

  • L⃗\vec{L}L is the total orbital angular momentum.

  • l⃗1,l⃗2,l⃗3\vec{l}_1, \vec{l}_2, \vec{l}_3l1​,l2​,l3​ are the individual angular momenta.

  • L^,l^i\hat{L}, \hat{l}_iL^,l^i​ are operators; L,liL, l_iL,li​ are the corresponding quantum numbers.

  • We assume a light atom and thus neglect the spin-orbit coupling.

Total Orbital Angular Momentum¶

Consider an atom with two electrons having orbital angular momenta l1l_1l1​ and l2l_2l2​. The maximum total angular momentum is obtained when the two vectors are parallel: L=l1+l2L = l_1 + l_2L=l1​+l2​. When they point in opposite directions, L=∣l1−l2∣L = |l_1 - l_2|L=∣l1​−l2​∣. Hence the total angular momentum quantum number LLL can take values (the “Clebsch-Gordan series”):

Quantization of orbital angular momentum LLL
L=l1+l2, l1+l2−1, …, ∣l1−l2∣{L = l_1 + l_2,\, l_1 + l_2 - 1,\, \ldots,\, \left|l_1 - l_2\right|}L=l1​+l2​,l1​+l2​−1,…,∣l1​−l2​∣
(4)

For example, if we have two electrons in ppp-orbitals, this gives L=2,1L = 2, 1L=2,1, or 0. Furthermore, for L=2L = 2L=2 we can have ML=+2,+1,0,−1,−2M_L = +2, +1, 0, -1, -2ML​=+2,+1,0,−1,−2; for L=1L = 1L=1, ML=+1,0,−1M_L = +1, 0, -1ML​=+1,0,−1; and for L=0L = 0L=0, ML=0M_L = 0ML​=0.

It is instructive to check that we have the same number of states in both representations (the uncoupled versus the coupled representation). In the uncoupled representation there are 32=93^2 = 932=9 states (3 ppp-orbitals and 2 electrons), and in the coupled representation 5+3+1=95 + 3 + 1 = 95+3+1=9.

Usually the closed-shell inner-core electrons are not included in the consideration, as they do not contribute to the end result.

Total Spin Angular Momentum¶

The total spin angular momentum operator for a many-electron atom is:

S^=∑i=1Ns^i{\hat{S} = \sum\limits_{i=1}^{N}\hat{s}_i}S^=i=1∑N​s^i​
(5)

and the zzz-component of the total spin operator is:

S^z=∑i=1Ns^z,i{\hat{S}_z = \sum\limits_{i=1}^{N}\hat{s}_{z,i}}S^z​=i=1∑N​s^z,i​
(6)

Here s^i\hat{s}_is^i​ and s^z,i\hat{s}_{z,i}s^z,i​ refer to the spin angular momenta of the individual electrons. The total quantum number MSM_SMS​ is:

MS=∑i=1Nms,i{M_S = \sum\limits_{i=1}^{N} m_{s,i}}MS​=i=1∑N​ms,i​
(7)

This value ranges from −S-S−S to SSS, and the total quantum number SSS is given by:

Quantization of spin angular momentum SSS
S=s1+s2, s1+s2−1, …, ∣s1−s2∣{S = s_1 + s_2,\, s_1 + s_2 - 1,\, \ldots,\, \left|s_1 - s_2\right|}S=s1​+s2​,s1​+s2​−1,…,∣s1​−s2​∣
(8)

For example, for two electrons, S=1S = 1S=1 (a “triplet state”) or S=0S = 0S=0 (a “singlet state”).

Total Angular Momentum (Combined Orbital and Spin)¶

The total angular momentum operator J^\hat{J}J^ is the vector sum of L^\hat{L}L^ and S^\hat{S}S^:

J^⃗=L^⃗+S^⃗{\vec{\hat{J}} = \vec{\hat{L}} + \vec{\hat{S}}}J^=L^+S^
(9)
J^z=L^z+S^z{\hat{J}_z = \hat{L}_z + \hat{S}_z}J^z​=L^z​+S^z​
(10)

The total quantum number JJJ, with its corresponding magnetic quantum number MJM_JMJ​, is given by:

Quantization of total angular momentum JJJ
J=L+S, L+S−1, …, ∣L−S∣{J = L + S,\, L + S - 1,\, \ldots,\, \left|L - S\right|}J=L+S,L+S−1,…,∣L−S∣
(11)
MJ=ML+MS{M_J = M_L + M_S}MJ​=ML​+MS​
(12)

The coupling scheme above is called the LSLSLS coupling or Russell-Saunders coupling.

This approach is only approximate when spin-orbit coupling is included in the Hamiltonian. Spin-orbit interaction arises from relativistic effects; here we simply think of it as coupling the orbital and spin angular momenta with some given magnitude (the “spin-orbit coupling constant”).

The spin-orbit effect is larger for heavier atoms. For these atoms the LSLSLS coupling scheme begins to break down and only JJJ remains a good quantum number. This means, for example, that one can no longer speak about singlet and triplet electronic states. The LSLSLS coupling scheme works reasonably well for the first two rows of the periodic table.

Atomic Terms and Selection Rules¶

The table above shows the various term symbols that can correspond to given electronic configurations. A term symbol contains information about the total orbital and spin angular momenta as well as the total angular momentum (J=L+SJ = L + SJ=L+S):

Term Symbols
2S+1LJ{^{2S+1}L_J}2S+1LJ​
(13)

  • SSS is the total spin.

  • LLL is the total orbital angular momentum.

  • JJJ is the total angular momentum.

Both 2S+12S+12S+1 and JJJ are written as numbers, while LLL is written as a letter: S for L=0L = 0L=0, P for L=1L = 1L=1, D for L=2L = 2L=2, and so on. The quantity 2S+12S+12S+1 is the spin multiplicity (1 = singlet, 2 = doublet, 3 = triplet, ...).

The term symbol specifies the ground-state electronic configuration exactly. Note that only the valence electrons contribute to the term symbol.

Because of electron, electron interactions and spin-orbit coupling, we expect a splitting of energy levels that can be described by various term symbols.

Example: term symbol for ground-state He

What is the atomic term symbol for the He atom in its ground state?

Solution

The electron configuration of He is 1s21s^21s2 (two electrons in the 1s1s1s orbital with opposite spins). First we obtain SSS. There are two possibilities: S=1S = 1S=1 (triplet) or S=0S = 0S=0 (singlet). Since we are interested in the ground state, both electrons are in the 1s1s1s orbital and must have opposite spins, giving a singlet state. Thus S=0S = 0S=0 and 2S+1=12S + 1 = 12S+1=1. Since both electrons reside in an sss-orbital, l1=l2=0l_1 = l_2 = 0l1​=l2​=0 and L=0L = 0L=0. The total momentum is J=L+S=0+0=0J = L + S = 0 + 0 = 0J=L+S=0+0=0. The term symbol is therefore 1^11S0_00​.

Example: lowest-lying terms of carbon

What are the lowest-lying term symbols for a carbon atom?

Solution

The electronic configuration for ground-state C is 1s22s22p21s^2 2s^2 2p^21s22s22p2. To get the possible lowest-lying states, we consider only the two ppp-electrons. From the spin rule we get S=12+12=1S = \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} = 1S=21​+21​=1 or S=0S = 0S=0. The first case corresponds to a triplet, the second to a singlet. The total orbital angular momentum quantum numbers are L=2,1,0L = 2, 1, 0L=2,1,0, which correspond to D, P, and S terms, respectively. Because the electrons must have opposite spins when they share the same orbital, some SSS and LLL combinations are not allowed:

  1. L=2L = 2L=2 (D term): One state (ML=−2M_L = -2ML​=−2) must have both electrons in the ppp-orbital with ml=−1m_l = -1ml​=−1. These electrons must have opposite spins, so the triplet (parallel spins) is not allowed. Hence for L=2L = 2L=2 only the singlet 1^11D is possible.

  2. L=1L = 1L=1 (P term): In principle these configurations could also be written for the singlet, but a more careful analysis shows that this is not allowed.

  3. L=0L = 0L=0 (S term): Here only ML=0M_L = 0ML​=0 is possible. Again the triplet is not possible because the spins would have to be parallel in the same orbital, so only 1^11S exists.

We conclude that the possible terms are 1^11D, 3^33P, and 1^11S. Hund’s rules (below) predict that the 3^33P term is the ground state. The total angular momentum quantum number JJJ for this state may be J=L+S=2,1J = L + S = 2, 1J=L+S=2,1, or 0. Due to spin-orbit coupling these states have different energies, and Hund’s rules predict that the J=0J = 0J=0 state lies lowest. Therefore the 3^33P0_00​ state is the ground state of the carbon atom.

The “L,SL, SL,S” method above is fast and convenient but does not always work and cannot show, for instance, that 1^11P does not exist. A more complete approach lists every possible electron configuration (microstate), labels each by its MLM_LML​ and MSM_SMS​ values, counts how many times each (ML,MS)(M_L, M_S)(ML​,MS​) combination appears, and decomposes the result into term symbols. The total number of microstates NNN is:

N=(2(2l+1))!n! (2(2l+1)−n)!{N = \frac{(2(2l+1))!}{n!\,(2(2l+1) - n)!}}N=n!(2(2l+1)−n)!(2(2l+1))!​
(14)

where nnn is the number of electrons and lll is the orbital angular momentum quantum number (1 for ppp-orbitals, 2 for ddd, and so on). Counting the (ML,MS)(M_L, M_S)(ML​,MS​) combinations and decomposing them into term symbols confirms the carbon result and shows explicitly that 1^11P does not exist.

Exercise: term symbols of oxygen

Carry out the microstate-counting procedure for the oxygen atom (4 electrons distributed over the 2p2p2p orbitals). What are the resulting atomic term symbols?

Hund’s Rules¶

Hund’s (partly empirical) rules are:

  • The term arising from the ground configuration with the maximum multiplicity 2S+12S+12S+1 lies lowest in energy.

  • For levels with the same multiplicity, the one with the maximum value of LLL lies lowest in energy.

  • For levels with the same SSS and LLL but different JJJ, the lowest-energy state depends on how much the subshell is filled:

    • If the subshell is less than half-filled, the state with the smallest JJJ is lowest in energy.

    • If the subshell is more than half-filled, the state with the largest JJJ is lowest in energy.

Spin-Orbit Interaction¶

This relativistic effect can be incorporated into non-relativistic quantum mechanics by adding the following term to the Hamiltonian:

H^SO=A L^⃗⋅S^⃗{\hat{H}_{SO} = A\,\vec{\hat{L}}\cdot \vec{\hat{S}}}H^SO​=AL^⋅S^
(15)

where AAA is the spin-orbit coupling constant and L^\hat{L}L^ and S^\hat{S}S^ are the orbital and spin angular momentum operators.

The total angular momentum JJJ commutes with both H^\hat{H}H^ and H^SO\hat{H}_{SO}H^SO​, so it can be specified simultaneously with the energy. We say that the quantum number JJJ remains good even when spin-orbit interaction is included, whereas LLL and SSS do not. The operator dot product L^⋅S^\hat{L}\cdot\hat{S}L^⋅S^ can be evaluated in terms of the quantum numbers:

Spin-orbit eigenvalue
L^⃗⋅S^⃗∣ψL,S,J⟩=12[J(J+1)−L(L+1)−S(S+1)]∣ψL,S,J⟩{\vec{\hat{L}}\cdot\vec{\hat{S}}\left|\psi_{L,S,J}\right\rangle = \frac{1}{2}\left[J(J+1)-L(L+1)-S(S+1)\right]\left|\psi_{L,S,J}\right\rangle}L^⋅S^∣ψL,S,J​⟩=21​[J(J+1)−L(L+1)−S(S+1)]∣ψL,S,J​⟩
(16)

For example, in alkali atoms (S=1/2S = 1/2S=1/2, L=1L = 1L=1) the spin-orbit interaction breaks the degeneracy of the excited 2^22P state into 2^22P3/2_{3/2}3/2​ and 2^22P1/2_{1/2}1/2​ (with 2^22S1/2_{1/2}1/2​ as the ground state).

Atomic Spectra and Selection Rules¶

The following selection rules for photon absorption or emission in one-electron atoms can be derived by considering the symmetries of the initial and final state wavefunctions:

Δn=unrestricted,Δl=±1,Δml=+1,0,−1{\Delta n = \textnormal{unrestricted},\quad \Delta l = \pm 1,\quad \Delta m_l = +1, 0, -1}Δn=unrestricted,Δl=±1,Δml​=+1,0,−1
(17)

where Δn\Delta nΔn is the change in the principal quantum number, Δl\Delta lΔl the change in orbital angular momentum, and Δml\Delta m_lΔml​ the change in its projection.

Qualitatively, the selection rules follow from conservation of angular momentum:

  • Photons are spin-1 particles with ml=+1m_l = +1ml​=+1 (left-circularly polarized light) or ml=−1m_l = -1ml​=−1 (right-circularly polarized light).

  • When a photon interacts with an atom, the angular momentum may change only by +1 or -1, exactly as in the selection rules above.

Selection Rules for Multi-Electron Atoms¶

  1. ΔL=0,±1\Delta L = 0, \pm 1ΔL=0,±1, except that a transition from L=0L = 0L=0 to L=0L = 0L=0 does not occur.

  2. Δl=±1\Delta l = \pm 1Δl=±1 for the electron that is being excited (or is responsible for fluorescence).

  3. ΔJ=0,±1\Delta J = 0, \pm 1ΔJ=0,±1, except that a transition from J=0J = 0J=0 to J=0J = 0J=0 does not occur.

  4. ΔS=0\Delta S = 0ΔS=0: the electron spin does not change in an optical transition. The opposite holds for magnetic resonance spectroscopy, which deals with changes in spin states.

In some exceptional cases these rules may be violated, but the resulting transitions are extremely weak (“forbidden transitions”). Because of the last rule, some excited triplet states can have very long lifetimes, since the transition to the ground singlet state is forbidden (metastable states).

The Nature of Light, Matter Interaction¶

Light is electromagnetic radiation, so it has both electric and magnetic components. The oscillating electric field drives transitions in optical spectroscopy (UV/Vis, fluorescence, IR), whereas the magnetic component drives transitions in magnetic resonance spectroscopy (NMR, EPR/ESR).

Photon emission from an atom (for example, fluorescence) is difficult to understand with the quantum mechanical machinery developed so far. The plain Schrodinger equation predicts that excited states in atoms would have infinite lifetime in vacuum. This is not observed in practice: atoms and molecules return to the ground state by emitting a photon. This transition is caused by fluctuations of the electromagnetic field in the vacuum.

Problems¶

Problem 1: Spin multiplicity of two electrons

Two electrons each have spin s=12s = \tfrac{1}{2}s=21​. Use the spin-coupling rule to enumerate the allowed values of the total spin quantum number SSS, the corresponding spin multiplicities 2S+12S+12S+1, and the allowed values of MSM_SMS​ for each. Show that the total number of spin states is 4.

Solution

The coupling rule gives S=s1+s2,…,∣s1−s2∣=1,0S = s_1 + s_2, \ldots, |s_1 - s_2| = 1, 0S=s1​+s2​,…,∣s1​−s2​∣=1,0.

  • S=1S = 1S=1 (triplet, 2S+1=32S+1 = 32S+1=3): MS=+1,0,−1M_S = +1, 0, -1MS​=+1,0,−1, giving 3 states.

  • S=0S = 0S=0 (singlet, 2S+1=12S+1 = 12S+1=1): MS=0M_S = 0MS​=0, giving 1 state.

The total is 3+1=43 + 1 = 43+1=4, which matches the 22=42^2 = 422=4 states of the uncoupled representation (αα\alpha\alphaαα, αβ\alpha\betaαβ, βα\beta\alphaβα, ββ\beta\betaββ).

Problem 2: Ground-state term symbol of nitrogen

Determine the ground-state term symbol of the nitrogen atom, whose valence configuration is 2p32p^32p3. Apply Hund’s rules in order.

Solution

With three ppp-electrons and three ppp-orbitals, the maximum-multiplicity arrangement (Hund’s first rule) puts one electron in each orbital with parallel spins:

MSmax⁡=12+12+12=32  ⇒  S=32,2S+1=4.M_S^{\max} = \tfrac{1}{2}+\tfrac{1}{2}+\tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{3}{2} \;\Rightarrow\; S = \tfrac{3}{2},\quad 2S+1 = 4.MSmax​=21​+21​+21​=23​⇒S=23​,2S+1=4.
(18)

With one electron in each of ml=+1,0,−1m_l = +1, 0, -1ml​=+1,0,−1, the total ML=+1+0−1=0M_L = +1+0-1 = 0ML​=+1+0−1=0, so L=0L = 0L=0 (an S term). The subshell is exactly half-filled, so the only value of JJJ is J=L+S=32J = L + S = \tfrac{3}{2}J=L+S=23​. The ground-state term symbol is 4^44S3/2_{3/2}3/2​.

Problem 3: Spin-orbit splitting of a 2^22P term

Using the eigenvalue of L^⃗⋅S^⃗\vec{\hat{L}}\cdot\vec{\hat{S}}L^⋅S^, compute the spin-orbit energy A⟨L^⃗⋅S^⃗⟩A\langle\vec{\hat{L}}\cdot\vec{\hat{S}}\rangleA⟨L^⋅S^⟩ for the two levels 2^22P3/2_{3/2}3/2​ and 2^22P1/2_{1/2}1/2​ of an alkali atom (L=1L=1L=1, S=12S=\tfrac{1}{2}S=21​). Which level lies lower for a less-than-half-filled subshell?

Solution

The eigenvalue is 12[J(J+1)−L(L+1)−S(S+1)]\tfrac{1}{2}[J(J+1) - L(L+1) - S(S+1)]21​[J(J+1)−L(L+1)−S(S+1)] with L(L+1)=2L(L+1) = 2L(L+1)=2 and S(S+1)=34S(S+1) = \tfrac{3}{4}S(S+1)=43​.

  • J=32J = \tfrac{3}{2}J=23​: 12[154−2−34]=12(1)=+12\tfrac{1}{2}\left[\tfrac{15}{4} - 2 - \tfrac{3}{4}\right] = \tfrac{1}{2}(1) = +\tfrac{1}{2}21​[415​−2−43​]=21​(1)=+21​, so ESO=+12AE_{SO} = +\tfrac{1}{2}AESO​=+21​A.

  • J=12J = \tfrac{1}{2}J=21​: 12[34−2−34]=12(−2)=−1\tfrac{1}{2}\left[\tfrac{3}{4} - 2 - \tfrac{3}{4}\right] = \tfrac{1}{2}(-2) = -121​[43​−2−43​]=21​(−2)=−1, so ESO=−AE_{SO} = -AESO​=−A.

For a less-than-half-filled subshell A>0A > 0A>0, so the J=12J = \tfrac{1}{2}J=21​ level lies lower, consistent with Hund’s third rule (smallest JJJ lowest).

Problem 4: Coupled-uncoupled state counting for 2p22p^22p2

For two equivalent ppp-electrons (l=1l = 1l=1), use the microstate formula to compute the total number of allowed microstates NNN. The allowed terms are 1^11S, 1^11D, and 3^33P. Verify that the number of states in these terms adds up to NNN.

Problem 5: Allowed and forbidden transitions in helium

Apply the multi-electron selection rules to decide which of the following helium transitions are allowed: (a) 1^11S0→1_0 \to {}^10​→1P1_11​, (b) 1^11S0→3_0 \to {}^30​→3P1_11​, (c) 3^33P1→3_1 \to {}^31​→3S1_11​, (d) 1^11S0→1_0 \to {}^10​→1S0_00​.

Problem 6: Ground-state term symbol of oxygen

Determine the ground-state term symbol of the oxygen atom, valence configuration 2p42p^42p4. Note that the subshell is more than half-filled, so Hund’s third rule selects the largest JJJ.

Solution

A 2p42p^42p4 configuration has the same terms as 2p22p^22p2 (a “hole” picture), namely 1^11S, 1^11D, and 3^33P, so the ground term is 3^33P with S=1S = 1S=1, L=1L = 1L=1. The possible JJJ values are J=L+S,…,∣L−S∣=2,1,0J = L+S, \ldots, |L-S| = 2, 1, 0J=L+S,…,∣L−S∣=2,1,0. Because the 2p2p2p subshell is more than half-filled (4 of 6 electrons), Hund’s third rule selects the largest JJJ, so J=2J = 2J=2. The ground-state term symbol is 3^33P2_22​.
